summary: I live in Longchang, Sichuan, where the hum of handlooms and the scent of dyed silk have been part of my daily rhythm since childhood. Our family workshop sits just behind our home, a modest space filled with wooden looms and stacks of carefully folded fabrics. Each morning, I rise early to check the threads and prepare the dyes, making sure every color and pattern honors the techniques passed down through generations. While some suggest embracing the latest machines, I prefer to keep the core of our craft untouched—there’s a certain soul in handwoven silk that no automation can replicate. Managing the family business means juggling many roles. After tending to the weaving, I spend hours negotiating with suppliers, always cautious to source materials that meet our standards for authenticity and sustainability. I use online platforms sparingly, mostly to find rare threads or tools that support our traditional methods. Evenings often bring family gatherings where stories of our ancestors’ weaving journeys are shared, reminding me why preserving this heritage matters so deeply. Outside the workshop, I enjoy attending local artisan fairs. These events are more than markets—they’re gatherings where I reconnect with fellow craftspeople and immerse myself in the rich tapestry of Sichuan’s cultural traditions. I savor simple, home-cooked meals—spicy tofu, fresh greens, and steamed rice—that fuel long days of work without distraction. When fatigue sets in, I take brief pauses, sometimes sipping jasmine tea while reflecting on the patterns I’m creating or the next steps for the business. Though I’m wary of rapid technological changes, I remain open to small innovations that help sustain our legacy without compromising quality. I find satisfaction in mentoring younger artisans, sharing not just techniques but the values of patience and respect that underpin our work. The delicate balance of honoring tradition while navigating modern pressures shapes my days, and I carry this responsibility quietly, one thread at a time.

summary: I live in Handan, a city in northern China’s Hebei Province, where the skyline is always changing and the hum of construction never really stops. My apartment sits high above the busy streets, outfitted with the latest appliances and a view I paid extra for—comfort is something I don’t compromise on. Most mornings, I start late, reaching for my phone before anything else. Coffee and a convenience store pastry are enough to get me going, especially if there’s a deadline looming. My work as a geological consultant keeps me moving between the city and the industrial sites scattered around the Taihang foothills. I’ve spent over fifteen years in this field, evaluating mineral resources and preparing technical reports for mining and construction projects. The job is demanding, but I’ve learned how to deliver results quickly, which means more lucrative contracts and bonuses. I prefer working alone, keeping interactions with colleagues and clients strictly professional. Office politics don’t interest me unless there’s something in it for me. When I’m not working, I’m usually planning my next trip or browsing online for the latest gadgets or home upgrades. Travel is a real pleasure—luxury hotels, good food, and the feeling of stepping away from routine. I don’t cook much at home; I’d rather try a new restaurant or order in, especially if it means more time to relax in front of the TV or scroll through shopping apps. I have little patience for group activities or family gatherings, and I rarely get involved in community events. My focus is on what makes my own life easier and more enjoyable. I don’t keep pets, and I don’t spend time on hobbies that don’t offer a clear reward. Even when it comes to environmental concerns or social causes, I only pay attention if it affects my work or comfort. For me, success is measured in upgrades—whether it’s a better apartment, a new phone, or a first-class ticket to my next vacation spot.
